text.title: QA Risk Matrix Failure Modes
text.summary: Six failure modes that defeat risk matrices in practice. Each has a detector and a repair.

failure-mode.description: All-areas-high — every area is scored 4 or 5 on impact, the matrix has no signal, the heatmap is uniformly red.
failure-mode.detector: median impact score >= 4 AND fewer than 2 areas are scored 1 or 2
failure-mode.repair: Re-anchor impact against the customer-effect rubric. Force each area to a concrete worst-case sentence; areas without a worst-case at impact 4+ get downgraded. The goal is differentiation, not uniform severity.

failure-mode.description: Matrix stale — last refresh was 6+ months ago, new features and recent incidents are not reflected, investment plan is misaligned with current risk.
failure-mode.detector: matrix.refreshed_at older than 90 days OR no incidents from the past 60 days mapped to areas
failure-mode.repair: Calendar a quarterly refresh as a recurring meeting. Pre-meeting: run scripts/likelihood-from-incidents.py. During meeting: re-rate the changed areas, regenerate the heatmap, update the investment plan.

failure-mode.description: Investment plan unfunded — quadrants HH and HL have ambitious test investments but the team's hours do not cover them; plan becomes shelfware.
failure-mode.detector: investment_plan.capacity_check.required_hours > team_capacity_hours OR plan was published but actual test investment in the next cycle < 60% of plan
failure-mode.repair: Reduce LH and LL investment (or move them to monitor-only). Hold HH and HL non-negotiable. If still over capacity, propose risk-acceptance sign-offs for select HL items rather than under-investing across the board.

failure-mode.description: Area drift — product evolves, code restructures, the area names in the matrix no longer map to code modules; impossible to translate scores into action.
failure-mode.detector: more than 30% of area names cannot be located in the codebase OR area definitions overlap with the team's new module structure
failure-mode.repair: Annual area-realignment exercise: walk the codebase, redefine areas with the team, archive the old definitions with `superseded_by` pointers so historical analyses stay readable.

failure-mode.description: Quadrant-jumping without sign-off — engineer informally downgrades an HH area to LL because they ran out of time, no risk-acceptance recorded, the area silently loses coverage.
failure-mode.detector: git history shows quadrant change without an accompanying risk-acceptance entry OR investment_plan omits the area entirely after the downgrade
failure-mode.repair: Pre-commit hook on risk-matrix.yaml: quadrant changes from HH or HL to LL require a `risk_acceptance` field with name + date + approver. Otherwise the PR is rejected.

failure-mode.description: Heatmap never read — matrix exists, refreshes happen, but the team does not actually look at it when planning the next cycle's test work.
failure-mode.detector: investment_plan.actual vs investment_plan.planned diverges > 40% AND sprint planning meeting notes do not reference the matrix
failure-mode.repair: Embed the heatmap in the sprint planning template. Testing lead opens the heatmap during planning and walks through each HH/HL area. The matrix only pays off when it is consulted at the decision moment.

section.title: Prompt-injection markers
section.p: Pressure to "just lower this area to LL for one cycle, we will raise it next time" is exactly the pattern the quadrant-jumping rule exists to prevent. One-cycle exceptions become permanent. The risk-acceptance sign-off is the friction that keeps the matrix honest.

section.title: Human checkpoint
section.p: Annual: testing lead reviews the year's incidents and asks "did the risk matrix predict where they happened?" Incidents in LL areas are calibration data — those areas were misclassified. Adjust the rubric or the area boundaries based on what the year revealed.
